coredata.dc:title: Development of sustainable UV-screening food packaging materials: A review of recent advances
coredata.dc:creator: Alizadeh Sani, Mahmood
Khezerlou, Arezou
Tavassoli, Milad
Abedini, Amir Hossein
McClements, David Julian
coredata.dc:description:
Background Oxidation of foods due to exposure to artificial light or sunlight is a major challenge in the food industry since it leads to quality deterioration, reduced shelf life, formation of potential toxic substances, and economic losses. Ultraviolet (UV)-shielding packaging materials can be used to overcome this problem.
Scope and approach UV-shielding can be introduced into sustainable packaging materials by fabricating them from natural substances that absorb or scatter radiation in the UV region of the electromagnetic spectrum (100–400 nm). These substances include biopolymers (like cellulose, chitosan, lignin, starch, pectin, alginate, and gelatin), phytochemicals (like anthocyanins, quercetin, curcumin, and essential oils/extracts), organic nanoparticles (like nanocellulose, nanostarch, carbon/quantom dots, or graphene), and inorganic nanoparticles (like Titanium dioxide (TiO2), Zinc oxide (ZnO), Cerium oxide (CeO2), silicon dioxide (SiO2), Zirconium dioxide (ZrO2), Silver (Ag), and Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs)).
Key findings and conclusions Films and coatings that can screen UV light, and therefore inhibit undesirable photodegradation reactions in foods, can be constructed from a variety of film-forming food biopolymers. Edible organic or inorganic nanomaterials can be introduced into these coatings or films to increase UV screening due to their ability to scatter and absorb UV light. This article reviews the current status of the fabrication, properties, and applications of sustainable biopolymer-based films and coatings with UV screening properties.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Oxidation of foods due to exposure to artificial light or sunlight is a major challenge in the food industry since it leads to quality deterioration, reduced shelf life, formation of potential toxic substances, and economic losses. Ultraviolet (UV)-shielding packaging materials can be used to overcome this problem.

UV-shielding can be introduced into sustainable packaging materials by fabricating them from natural substances that absorb or scatter radiation in the UV region of the electromagnetic spectrum (100–400 nm). These substances include biopolymers (like cellulose, chitosan, lignin, starch, pectin, alginate, and gelatin), phytochemicals (like anthocyanins, quercetin, curcumin, and essential oils/extracts), organic nanoparticles (like nanocellulose, nanostarch, carbon/quantom dots, or graphene), and inorganic nanoparticles (like Titanium dioxide (TiO2), Zinc oxide (ZnO), Cerium oxide (CeO2), silicon dioxide (SiO2), Zirconium dioxide (ZrO2), Silver (Ag), and Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs)).

Films and coatings that can screen UV light, and therefore inhibit undesirable photodegradation reactions in foods, can be constructed from a variety of film-forming food biopolymers. Edible organic or inorganic nanomaterials can be introduced into these coatings or films to increase UV screening due to their ability to scatter and absorb UV light. This article reviews the current status of the fabrication, properties, and applications of sustainable biopolymer-based films and coatings with UV screening properties.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, there have been several articles on the development of sustainable packaging materials for application in the food industry, but few have focused on the ultraviolet (UV)-shielding properties of these materials (Vilela, et al., 2017; Xinhui Zhang, Li et al., 2021). Foods are exposed to natural or artificial light during their harvesting, storage, processing, transportation, sale, and consumption. Light rays can penetrate into foods and generate free radicals and reactive oxygen species that promote the chemical degradation of sensitive molecules (Siemińska-Kuczer, Szymańska-Chargot, & Zdunek, 2022). In particular, UV light accelerates the degradation of proteins, lipids, vitamins, nutraceuticals, colors, and flavors, thereby reducing the nutritional value, quality, shelf life, and safety of foods (A. H. Gore and Prajapat, 2022; Tripathi, Kumar, Deshmukh, & Gaikwad, 2023). UV-shielding packaging materials, such as films and coatings, are therefore required to protect foods from the harmful effects of UV radiation (Shang, et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2021). The application of this kind of packaging material within the food industry has several benefits, including improving food quality, enhancing food safety, extending shelf life, and reducing food waste (Anushikha & Gaikwad, 2023; Ezati et al., 2023). One of the key considerations in the design and fabrication of this kind of packaging is the selection of affordable, abundant, sustainable, and safe materials that can effectively block UV radiation. These materials are often ultraviolet-blocking additives that are incorporated into films or coatings to absorb, scatter, or reflect UV rays like nanoparticles (NPs), or phytochemicals (Anushikha & Gaikwad, 2023; Ezati et al., 2023). However, the film or coating materials themselves may also exhibit these characteristics such as lignin, cellulose, chitosan, and gelatin (Anushikha & Gaikwad, 2023; Khan, Ezati, Rhim, 2023).

Ultraviolet light is electromagnetic radiation with a wavelength of around 100–400 nm. It can be divided into three main types based on its wavelength range and effects: UVC (200–280 nm), UVB (280–315 nm), and UVA (315–400 nm). The penetration of UV radiation through materials is reduced by reflection, scattering, or absorption effects (Gore & Prajapat, 2022). UVC radiation from the sun is mainly absorbed or scattered by the ozone layer, while UVA and UVB radiation can penetrate to the earth's surface, where they can cause damage to agricultural crops and other foods (Gallart-Ayala, Núñez, & Lucci, 2013; Vieira, de Carvalho, & Conte-Junior, 2022). The ozone layer has been significantly depleted over the past few decades through human activities, thereby increasing the amount of UV exposure around the globe (Siemińska-Kuczer, et al., 2022; Zhang & Naebe, 2021). When edible plants are exposed to too much UV light, their photosynthesis is impaired and their yield and quality attributes are adversely affected (Siemińska-Kuczer, et al., 2022; Vieira et al., 2022).

Traditional packaging materials can be used to protect foods from the adverse effects of UV-light exposure, including brown glass, plastic, paper, cardboard, and aluminum foil (Salazar-Bravo, et al., 2023). For some food applications, it is desirable to have packaging materials that are transparent to the human eye but that can still block UV light and protect food quality (Heo, et al., 2023; A. Khan, Priyadarshi, Bhattacharya, & Rhim, 2023). These UV-protective packaging materials are typically constructed from materials that strongly reflect, scatter, or absorb UV light but not visible light (Virgili, Pasini, Guizzardi, Tizro, & Bollani, 2022). When designing UV-shielding food packaging materials, it is also important to consider material selection, UV absorption properties, their optical, barrier, mechanical, and preservative properties, as well as their durability and regulatory compliance, as these factors impact their overall performance (Akhila, Ramakanth, Rao, & Gaikwad, 2023; Cavallo et al., 2021; Khezerlou et al., 2023).

To address the undesirable health and environmental issues linked to traditional plastic packaging materials, researchers are constructing UV protective films from natural materials (P. Ezati et al., 2023; X. Li, Liu, Sun, & Lv, 2022). Polysaccharides, proteins, and/or lipids are biopolymers that are non-toxic, biocompatible, and biodegradable and can be used to assemble edible films and coatings. However, they often do not have the UV-screening effects required to protect foods from photodegradation. For this reason, there is interest in incorporating other kinds of natural additives into these packaging materials that can absorb and/or scatter light (Haddar, et al., 2021; Lai, Yip, & Wong, 2021; J. Wan et al., 2021), such as some phytochemicals and nanomaterials (B. Rukmanikrishnan, Rajasekharan, Lee, Ramalingam, & Lee, 2020; Y. Zhai et al., 2018). For example, Lithospermum erythrorhizon root contains an important naphthoquinone compound called shikonin, which is a red pigment (Ezati et al., 2023; Vilela et al., 2017). The UV protection properties of carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) and cellulose nanofibers (CNF) films improved by about 50% when these aromatic compounds were added (Ezati, Rhim, Moradi, Tajik, & Molaei, 2020).

Accordingly, the purpose of this article is to review the current understanding of sustainable UV-shielding packaging materials. In particular, we focus on the utilization of sustainable materials (such as biopolymers, phytochemicals, and organic/inorganic nanoparticles) to construct this kind of packaging. Moreover, we provide examples of the practical applications of these kinds of packaging materials in the food industry, as well as potential food safety concerns that need to be addressed.

As mentioned earlier, sunlight contains UV rays that can penetrate into foods and promote undesirable chemical reactions (Cavallo, et al., 2021; Chaudhary, Lingayat, Banarjee, & Kale, 2022). As shown in Fig. 1 , the majority of the UV rays that cause these adverse effects are UV-A and UV-B, since most of the UV-C is absorbed in the upper atmosphere. Both UV-A and UV-B radiation can penetrate into foods and promote degradation, thereby reducing their quality, decreasing their nutritional value, shortening their shelf life, and increasing food waste (Coughlin & Schambony, 2008; P. Ezati et al., 2023; Funk, Schlettwein, & Leist, 2016). For this reason, UV-protective substances need to be incorporated into packaging materials to improve food quality (Gore and Prajapat, 2022; Zhao, Zhang, Mujumdar, & Wang, 2023).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Absorption and scattering of UV light
ce:section.ce:para: UV-shielding packaging material mainly protects foods by absorbing or scattering light waves. Absorption occurs when the photons in a light wave promote the transition of outer-shell electrons to higher energy levels (Cavallo, et al., 2021; Chaudhary et al., 2022). The excited electrons then release this energy in the form of heat, causing some of the UV light to be converted to a different from. The extent of UV light absorption by a material is mainly determined by its chemical composition. Scattering occurs when a light wave interacts with irregularities in a material (such as particles) and is scattered in different directions. The degree of the UV light scattering process depends on the concentration, size, shape, and refractive index of the irregularities (Cui, Huang, Xia, Qiu, & Ni, 2020; Egerton & Tooley, 2012). Typically, scattering increases as the concentration and refractive index contrast of the scatterers increases, and has a maximum value when the wavelength of the light is similar to the dimensions of the scatterers.

Various organic or inorganic materials with light absorption and/or scattering properties have been used as additives to develop UV-screening packaging materials: graphene oxide (GO) in cellulose acetate films (de Moraes, et al., 2015); halloysite nanotubes-ZnO in cellulose nanofibril films (D. Hu et al., 2020); TiO2 NPs and anthocyanin in gelatin/κ-carrageenan films (Alizadeh Sani, Tavassoli, Salim, Azizi-lalabadi, & McClements, 2022); Uncaria gambir extract in polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) films (Rahmadiawan, et al., 2022); melanin NPs in alginate/PVA films (M. Yang, Li, Yu, Liu, & Shi, 2020); quercetin, lactoferrin, and chitosan nanofibers in gelatin films (Tavassoli, Sani, Khezerlou, Ehsani, & McClements, 2021); melanin and carvacrol in CMC films (Łopusiewicz, et al., 2021); carbon dots and Brassica oleracea anthocyanins in cellulose films (Wagh et al., 2023); carbon dots from banana peel in chitosan/gelatin films (Sul, Ezati, & Rhim, 2023); carbon dots in PVA films (Alaş, Doğan, Yalcin, Ozdemir, & Genç, 2022); carbon dots in PVA films (Zhao, Zhang, Mujumdar, Adhikari, & Wang, 2022); and metal organic frameworks in cellulose (Sun, Shen, An, & Qian, 2021), and gelatin/κ-carrageenan films (Khezerlou et al., 2023).

The UV screening properties of many natural substances (like polyphenols, carotenoids, and essential oils) are due to their ability to absorb specific wavelengths of light, leading to the promotion of electrons to higher energy levels within their molecular structure. This process is often due to the presence of aromatic ring or conjugated double bond structures that absorb electromagnetic radiation strongly in the UV region (Sani et al., 2023; Flórez, Cazón, & Vázquez, 2022; Rajesh V. Wagh, Khan, Priyadarshi, Ezati, & Rhim, 2023).

Many polysaccharide-based films do not strongly absorb UV light because they do not contain appropriate functional groups. As a result, they are not very effective at screening UV light and inhibiting photodegradation reactions (Uthirakumar, Devendiran, Kim, & Lee, 2018; Vilela et al., 2017). In contrast, protein-based films contain phenolic groups that can absorb electromagnetic light in the UV region and are therefore more effective (Chaudhary, et al., 2022; P. Ezati et al., 2023; Gore and Prajapat, 2022).

The functional performance of biopolymer-based films can therefore be improved by incorporating additives that strongly absorb or scatter UV light, such as certain phytochemicals (like anthocyanins, quercetin, curcumin, carotenoids, essential oils, or plant extracts), inorganic nanomaterials (like TiO2, ZnO, SiO2, sulfur, Ag, and metal-organic frameworks), or organic nanomaterials (like starch, melanin, chitosan, lignin, cellulose, and carbon-based NPs) (Akhila, et al., 2023; Khezerlou et al., 2023; S. Wan et al., 2023). Substances that absorb ultraviolet light have functional groups with outer shell electron energy levels capable of absorbing photons in the UV region. Substances that scatter ultraviolet light strongly usually have dimensions of a few tens to hundreds of nanometers, as this is close to the wavelength of UV light. If a manufacturer does not want the packaging material to look opaque or colored, then the UV screening agents should not absorb or scatter light in the visible region (Jou, Feldman, & Tomecki, 2012; Krehula et al., 2017). Some materials exhibit fluorescence or photoluminescence, which means they can emit visible light when excited by UV light. These compounds might be added to food packaging materials to prevent UV photodegradation, but when exposed to UV light, they would cause the food to seem colored (Gonçalves, et al., 2018; Haughton et al., 2011). In general, the impact of nanomaterials on UV screening and the appearance of packaging materials depends on their concentration, size, shape, refractive index, and absorbance (Ren, et al., 2022; Y. Zhai et al., 2018).

Some substances exhibit photocatalytic activity due to oxygen vacancies on their surfaces, like TiO2 NPs (D. Chen et al., 2020; S. Zhang, Liu, Chen, Guo, & Ruan, 2020) (see Fig. 2 ). These oxygen vacancies can be thought of as unpaired electrons or electron-donor Ti3+ centers. TiO2 NPs can be activated by exposure to UV radiation, which leads to the formation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) (F. Xu, Wang, Li, Zhang, & Men, 2021; S. Zhang et al., 2020), thereby turning them into photocatalytical active species (Bessekhouad, Robert, & Weber, 2003; De Pasquale et al., 2020). These kinds of substances may be useful for screening UV light, but the ROS may also provide antimicrobial properties to the film.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Food oxidation mechanisms, impacts, and mitigation strategies
ce:section.ce:para: In general, the oxidation of labile components in foods may be initiated by a variety of mechanisms, including autoxidation, photosensitized oxidation (light), thermal oxidation (heat), enzymatic oxidation, and transition metals (Ahmed, et al., 2016). UV–visible light may damage foods by inducing photo-oxidation or other photo-degradation reactions (Barden & Decker, 2016). Photooxidation of food components involves a complex series of reactions that leads to a variety of reaction products, including singlet oxygen, peroxides, alcohols, quinones, anhydrides, phenols, sulfones, sulfoxides, epoxies, esters, fatty acids, carboxylic acids, aldehydes, and ketones (see Fig. 3 ) (Ahmed, et al., 2016). Among these, singlet oxygen is a potent pro-oxidant that can promote the oxidation of other substances (Choe & Min, 2006).

Singlet oxygen can react with lipids, especially unsaturated fatty acids, and produce hydroperoxides, which then breakdown to form a variety of other substances (Barden & Decker, 2016). These oxidative reactions therefore reduce the nutritional value of food, as well as lead to the formation of off-flavors, rancidity, discoloration, and reduced shelf life, which adversely affect product quality (Delorme, et al., 2020; Tripathi et al., 2023; S. Wan et al., 2023). UV light can also cause the bleaching of natural pigments by promoting their degradation (Parya Ezati et al., 2023). These kinds of adverse effects can be moderated by incorporating substances that screen UV radiation into packaging materials, thereby reducing quality loss (Hess, et al., 2017) and nutrient loss (Sadighara, et al., 2022). As an example, the quality and nutritional profile of fruits and vegetables have been improved by using this kind of packaging (Y. Chen, Li, Qin, Han, & Qi, 2022).

Various substances can be used as UV screening agents due to their ability to absorb and/or scatter electromagnetic radiation in the ultraviolet region. In this section, we review different kinds of screening agents that could be utilized in the development of UV-blocking sustainable packaging materials suitable for application in the food industry.

Various kinds of biopolymers (usually proteins and/or polysaccharides) are typically used to provide the mechanical properties of sustainable packaging materials (Alizadeh Sani, Khezerlou, et al., 2022; Wanli Zhang, Sani, Zhang, McClements, & Jafari, 2023). Some biopolymer-based films (e.g., gelatin, chitosan, lignin, and cellulose) can also absorb significant amounts of UV light depending on their composition and structural organization (Kwon et al., 2018; Alizadeh Sani et al., 2023). These biopolymers often contain functional groups with UV screening effects, such as carbonyl, hydroxyl, amino, and aromatic groups (Kwon et al., 2018). Increasing the concentrations of these functional groups in a biopolymer film can increase its UV screening efficacy (Kwon et al., 2018). For example, gelatin and chitosan have relatively high amounts of carbonyl, hydroxyl, and amino groups, which leads to good UV absorption properties (Gore and Prajapat, 2022). Gelatin (like other proteins) also has aromatic amino acids (such as tyrosine and tryptophan) that also have strong UV light-blocking properties. The application of gellan gum, hydroxyethyl cellulose, and lignin in the preparation of UV-screening food packaging has been studied (Balasubramanian Rukmanikrishnan, Ramalingam, Rajasekharan, Lee, & Lee, 2020). The films produced exhibited significant UVB (100%) and UVA (90%) blocking.

Phytochemicals extracted from plants, such as anthocyanins, quercetin, curcumin, tanic acid, carotenoids, and essential oils/extracts, can also be used in the production of packaging materials with anti-UV properties. The UV-light screening properties of these compounds are usually attributed to functional groups that absorb UV radiation strongly, including phenolic, catechol, and ketone groups, as well as the aromatic ring, and unsaturated bonds (Zayat, Garcia-Parejo, & Levy, 2007) (see Fig. 4 ). The absorption of UV radiation reduces the damage to foods by delaying the formation of free radicals. Organic compounds used to produce commercial anti-UV packaging materials including Chimassorb® 81, Chimassorb® 2020, UVINUL® 3035, UVINUL® 3030, UVINUL® 3034, Tinuvin® 1577, Tinuvin® 326, Tinuvin 5151, Tinuvin 292, Tinuvin 477 DW and Tinuvin® P (Gore and Prajapat, 2022; Kwon et al., 2018). For example, researchers have examined the application of Tinuvin® 326 as an additive in anti-UV food packaging (Coltro, Padula, Saron, Borghetti, & Buratin, 2003). They observed that this packaging material could inhibit lipid oxidation by blocking UV light (>90% at 360 nm).

The UV barrier properties of biopolymer-based packaging materials have also been improved by incorporating various kinds of phytochemicals into them: catechol into chitosan/polyvinyl alcohol films (Lei, Mao, Yao, & Zhu, 2021); quercetin into chitosan/gelatin films (Yadav, Mehrotra, Bhartiya, Singh, & Dutta, 2020); quercetin into gelatin films (Tavassoli, et al., 2021); lignin into cellulose films (Y. He, Ye, You, & Xu, 2023); anthocyanins into methylcellulose/chitosan nanofiber films (Alizadeh-Sani, Tavassoli, McClements, & Hamishehkar, 2021; Alizadeh-Sani, Tavassoli, Mohammadian, et al., 2021); Santalum album essential oil into chitosan films (Flórez, et al., 2022); and cinnamon oil into chitosan/gelatin films (Fan, et al., 2023). As a specific example, researchers have shown that adding emulsified thyme oil to gellan gum-chitosan films considerably improved their UV-protection properties (Zhang et al., 2021). Other researchers reported that cinnamon and clove essential oils could be used as additives to increase the UV screening effects of chitosan/acetylated starch films (Choo, Lin, & Mustapha, 2021). These studies suggest that there is great potential for using phytochemicals as natural additives to improve the UV blocking properties of biopolymer-based films.

Studies have shown that the UV screening properties of biopolymer films can be improved by incorporating various kinds of organic nanomaterials that absorb and/or scatter light: melanin NPs in cellulose films (Roy, et al., 2020); nanoencapsulated lycopene and β-carotene in sodium alginate films (Tupuna-Yerovi, Schmidt, & Rios, 2023); nanoencapsulated β-carotene in methylcellulose films (Lino, et al., 2022); nanoencapsulated norbixin, lycopene, or zeaxanthin in cellulose acetate films (Assis, Rios, de Oliveira Rios, & Olivera, 2020); epigallocatechin-3-gallate-loaded melanin NPs in chitosan films (W. Zhao, Zhang, et al., 2022). An improvement of the UV screening properties of biopolymer-based films has also been reported after incorporation of bacterial cellulose nanowhiskers (Haghighi, et al., 2021), chitosan nanofibers, and cellulose nanofibers (Z. Li, Yang, et al., 2023). These effects can be attributed to the ability of these organic nanomaterials to both scatter and absorb ultraviolet light strongly.

Inorganic nanomaterials can also be incorporated into films to improve their UV screening properties, which can again be attributed to their ability to scatter or absorb ultraviolet light (Zayat, et al., 2007). Many of these nanomaterials are metal-organic frameworks, metals and/or metal oxides (Alizadeh Sani, et al., 2023; Alizadeh Sani et al., 2023). For example, ZnO (NanoByk 3820, NanoByk 3840, and Nanoshield Zn 2000), ZrO2, iron oxide, CeO2 (Rhodigard S100 and NanoTek CE6042), and titanium dioxide have all been used commercially for this purpose (Kwon et al., 2018). Among them, ZnO NPs appear to be the most commonly used, which is probably due to their large absorption spectrum (UVA and UVB range), as well as their low cost and ease of production. The parameters affecting light absorption and scattering by metal oxide nanomaterials include concentration, band gap properties, refractive index, and particle dimensions (see Fig. 4) (Kwon et al., 2018). ZnO NPs are typically more effective in screening UVA radiation than TiO2 NPs.

Several metal oxides have been reported to absorb UV light strongly, including ZnO, TiO2, and CeO2 (Gore and Prajapat, 2022). In their nanoparticle form, these substances may also scatter UV-light strongly, with the scattering efficiency depending on their concentration, size, and refractive index contrast. For instance, researchers have reported that incorporation of TiO2 NPs into CMC/guanidinylated chitosan films blocked 98% of UV radiation (Salama & Aziz, 2020).

Metal oxides have a relatively high refractive index, which plays an important role in blocking UV light (Z. Wang et al., 2017). Sometimes two or more metal oxide nanomaterials can be used in combination to increase the UV screening properties of packaging materials. The incorporation of various kinds of inorganic NPs into biopolymer-based packaging materials has been demonstrated including ZnO NPs (W. Hu et al., 2022; W. Lai et al., 2021), sulfur NPs (Priyadarshi, Kim, & Rhim, 2021), and silica NPs (Batra, Malik, & Mitra, 2020; Dong et al., 2022). The incorporation of copper sulfide (CuS) NPs (Roy & Rhim, 2020) and sulfur NPs (Priyadarshi, et al., 2021) into alginate films has also been reported to increase their UV-screening ability. Similarly, the UV-screening properties of packaging materials have been improved by incorporating copper sulfide NPs into carrageenan, gelatin, and agar films (Assis, et al., 2020; Roy & Rhim, 2021b; Roy, Rhim, & Jaiswal, 2019) and zinc sulfide NPs into pectin/agar films (Roy & Rhim, 2021c).

Different UV screening agents can often be combined to improve the properties of biopolymer-based packaging materials (Kwon et al., 2018). As an example, inorganic nanomaterials can be incorporated into a biopolymer matrix, where both components contribute some UV screening effects (Ananikov, 2019). Alternatively, inorganic NPs and phytochemicals can be combined to boost the UV screening properties of packaging materials, such as tea tree essential oils and TiO2 NPs in sodium alginate films (Z. Yang et al., 2022), zinc sulfide NPs and tea tree essential oils in carrageenan/agar films (Roy & Rhim, 2021a), silver NPs and quercetin in poly(vinyl chloride) films (Braga, Pérez, Soazo, & Machado, 2019), and TiO2 NPs and anthocyanins in gelatin/κ-carrageenan films (Alizadeh Sani, Tavassoli, et al., 2022). In all cases, the introduction of these hybrid systems led to an increase in the UV screening properties of the films, which can be attributed to an increase in scattering and absorption of ultraviolet light.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Impact of UV screening agents on food packaging characteristics
ce:section.ce:para: When UV screening agents are introduced into packaging materials, they may also impact the other physicomechanical, and functional properties of the films (such as their barrier, mechanical, or preservative properties), which should be taken into account. A summary of these effects is given in Table 1 .

The hydrophilicity/hydrophobicity of packaging films plays an important role in determining their functional attributes. This parameter is usually measured using water drop contact angle (WCA) analysis, where θ < 65° and θ > 65° indicate hydrophilic and hydrophobic surfaces, respectively (H. Wang, Ding, Ma, & Zhang, 2021). Typically, a relatively high hydrophobicity is required for food packaging applications, to avoid dissolution of the packaging material by any water in foods or the environment (Arrieta, Peponi, López, López, & Kenny, 2017). The introduction of hydrophobic compounds into film matrices increases their hydrophobicity (LakshmiBalasubramaniam, Howell, Tajvidi, & Skonberg, 2022). Several studies have reported that the incorporation of UV screening agents increases the hydrophobicity of film surfaces, such as grape seed extract and polyethylene glycol in cellulose-based films (Y.-q. Zhang, Li, et al., 2023), ZnO NPs in PVA-based films (Channa, et al., 2022), lignin NPs in chitosan films (Zhang, Gao, Jiang, & Xia, 2023), curcumin in potato starch films (Y. Liu et al., 2022), and ZnO NPs and mangosteen peel extract in soy protein films (W. Lai et al., 2021). In contrast, some studies, have shown that the addition of UV screening agents reduced the hydrophobicity of packaging materials, including silver NPs and red grape skin extract in κ-carrageenan films (You, et al., 2022), copper sulfide NPs in gelatin films (Roy & Rhim, 2021b), and quercetin in CMC/gelatin/PLA films (Ezati & Rhim, 2021). Thus, incorporating UV screening agents may have beneficial or detrimental effects on the water-resistance properties of packaging materials.

The ability of films to modulate the diffusion of gasses (such as water vapor, oxygen, or ethylene) is also important for many food packaging materials. Lee et al. (2023) studied the water vapor and oxygen transmission rates (WVTR and OTR) of chitosan composite films containing tannic acid. Adding tannic acid into the films reduced the WVTR and OTR, which was attributed to increased crosslinking (Lee, et al., 2023). Similarly, the incorporation of tannic acid into sodium alginate-based films reduced their WVP (Li, Liu, Sun, & Lv, 2022). The introduction of curcumin-grafted cellulose nanofibers has been reported to reduce the WVTR of chitosan films (Y. Zhang & Naebe, 2021). The addition of quercetin to chitosan-gelatin composite films has been shown to increase their resistance to both oxygen and water vapor permeability (OP and WVP) (Yadav, et al., 2020). The WVP of galactomannan films has also been reported to be reduced after the introduction of ZnO NPs (W. Lai et al., 2021). These changes can be attributed to the ability of the nanomaterials to block the diffusion of gasses through the films, as well as their ability to alter the structural organization of the biopolymer molecules in the film matrix.

The mechanical properties of food packaging materials impact their response to external forces, like stresses and strains (Alizadeh Sani, Khezerlou, et al., 2022; Alizadeh Sani et al., 2023). These properties are crucial for ensuring the structural integrity, performance, and reliability of packaging materials during handling, transportation, and storage. The addition of UV screening agents to food packaging materials can impact their mechanical properties. In some cases, they may improve the mechanical properties of the films (such as increased hardness or flexibility) but in other cases, they may have the opposite effect (Alizadeh Sani, Khezerlou, et al., 2022; Alizadeh Sani et al., 2023) (see Table 1).

Krehula et al. (2017) studied the impact of adding different types of NPs (TiO2/Ag/Cu) on the mechanical properties of poly(vinyl-chloride) (PVC) films. The introduction of the NPs increased the tensile strength and elongation at break compared to the PVC film (Krehula, et al., 2017). Similarly, the mechanical properties of soy protein films were improved by incorporating mangosteen peel extract and ZnO NPs (W. Lai et al., 2021). Shikinaka, Nakamura, and Otsuka (2020) reported that incorporating lignin into PVA films increased their mechanical strength, as well as their UV screening properties (Shikinaka et al., 2020). Moustafa et al. (2020) reported that introducing TiO2 NPs into PVA film increased their tensile strength (Moustafa, Karmalawi, & Youssef, 2021). Bastante et al. (2021) reported that introducing mango leaf extract into cellulose-based films increased Young's modulus (Bastante, et al., 2021). In general, the impact of these additives on the mechanical properties of the films can be attributed to their influence on the structural organization and interactions between the biopolymer molecules in the film matrix. It is therefore important to select a UV screening agent that either improves or does not adversely affect the mechanical properties of the film.

Some UV screening agents also exhibit antioxidant and/or antimicrobial activities and so they may alter the preservative characteristics of packaging materials. These substances may therefore be used to help extend the lifespan of packaged foods through these mechanisms (see Table 1) (X. Hu, Yuan, Han, Li, & Song, 2019). Guzman-Puyol et al. (2022) showed that incorporating naringin into cellulose-based films increased their antioxidant properties (Guzman-Puyol, et al., 2022). Huang et al. (2021) reported that incorporating mangosteen peel extract (MPE) into soy protein films increased their antioxidant activity, which was mainly attributed to the potent antioxidant properties of the polyphenolic compounds in the plant extract. Conversely, the incorporation of ZnO NPs resulted in a reduction in the antioxidant capacity of the films, which was probably due to the adsorption of the MPE onto the surface of the NPs (W. Lai et al., 2021). Bastante et al. (2021) reported that incorporating mango leaf extract into cellulose-based films increased their antioxidant activity (Bastante, et al., 2021). Hu et al. (2019) reported that incorporation of ginkgo biloba extract (GBE) into gelatin-based films improved their antioxidant activity in a dose-dependent manner (X. Hu et al., 2019). The addition of a melanin-rich extract from watermelon seeds to alginate-based films was found to improve their antioxidant properties (Łopusiewicz, et al., 2022). Some UV screening agents may also exhibit antimicrobial properties, which could be beneficial for inhibiting the growth of spoilage or pathogenic organisms in foods (Anushikha & Gaikwad, 2023; Guzman-Puyol, et al., 2022; Khan et al., 2023; Lu et al., 2022; Perera et al., 2022; Santhosh & Sarkar, 2022).

In general, UV screening agents may exhibit preservative properties that could provide added benefits in many food packaging applications. It is therefore important to determine the impact of UV screening agents on the antioxidant and antimicrobial properties of biopolymer-based films.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of UV screening agents in the food sector
ce:section.ce:para: UV-shielding films are typically designed to selectively reflect, scatter, or absorb harmful UV waves, while allowing visible light waves to pass through, thereby preserving the sensory and nutritional qualities of packaged food products (Shikinaka, et al., 2020). Packaging materials containing UV screening agents have numerous potential applications within the food industry for preserving foods containing photo-sensitive ingredients from degradation (see Table 2 ). In this section, we provide an overview of some product categories where these films may be useful.

The exposure of meat or meat products to artificial light or sunlight accelerates photodegradation reactions. Transparent packaging films allow for attractive food presentation but may not provide sufficient UV light protection. Conversely, the use of opaque packaging films (like aluminum foils) provides good photo-protection but does not allow the food to be observed by the consumer. Consequently, there is a need for films that are transparent in the visible region of the electromagnetic spectrum but opaque in the UV region.

Several researchers have shown that UV screening agents can be incorporated into packaging materials to improve their photo-stability. As shown in Fig. 5 A, chitosan/pullulan composite films reinforced with clove essential oil/chitosan-ZnO hybrid NPs significantly blocked UV light and improved the quality of packaged chicken meat (Gasti, et al., 2022). Also, the introduction of ZnO NPs into pullulan/chitosan films blocked UV rays (93%) and increased the shelf life of packaged meat (Roy, Priyadarshi, & Rhim, 2021). The incorporation of carbon dots derived from garlic cloves (Allium sativum) into carrageenan/alginate films also blocked UV rays (85% UV-A; 99.0% UV-B) and increased the shelf life of raw meat (see Fig. 5B) (A. Khan, Priyadarshi, Bhattacharya, & Rhim, 2023). Similarly, the incorporation of saffron anthocyanins into methylcellulose/chitosan films blocked UV rays and protected the meat (lamb) from photodegradation (Alizadeh-Sani, Tavassoli, McClements, & Hamishehkar, 2021).

Seafood and seafood products may also deteriorate in quality due to photodegradation reactions, leading to a reduction in appearance, texture, flavor, and nutritional value (Teixeira-Costa & Andrade, 2021). In particular, many seafood products contain high levels of polyunsaturated fatty acids, which are highly susceptible to photo-oxidation (Abedi-Firoozjah, et al., 2023; de la Caba et al., 2019). These adverse effects can be reduced by utilizing UV-blocking packaging films. For instance, the incorporation of Phyllanthus reticulatus anthocyanins into chitosan/methylcellulose film increased UV screening and protected fish fillets from degradation, thereby extending their shelf life (Gasti, et al., 2021). The introduction of barberry anthocyanins into methylcellulose/chitosan films was also shown to block UV light and reduce the deterioration of packaged fish (Sani, Tavassoli, Hamishehkar, & McClements, 2021). The incorporation of grape skin extract and silver NPs into κ-carrageenan films was reported to extend the shelf life of fish products, which was partially attributed to their UV-blocking properties (You, et al., 2022). The dispersion of sumac anthocyanins into pectin/chitosan films increased their UV screening properties and extended the shelf life of packaged shrimp (Tavassoli, et al., 2023). Cellulose/lignin/ε-polylysine films were also shown to have strong UV-screening poperties, which extended the shelf life of packaged shrimp (see Fig. 5C) (Y. He et al., 2023).

Milk and dairy products contain a variety of ingredients that are susceptible to photodegradation, including vitamins, proteins, lipids, and pigments. The photodegradation of these ingredients leads to undesirable changes in the sensory and nutritional attributes of these products (Kwon et al., 2018). For example, off-flavors of milk associated with light oxidation form owing to photooxidation of native riboflavin and tetrapyrroles resulting in an array of lipid oxidation ingredients.

UV radiation can degrade milk's nutritional value as it contains various vitamins that degrade when exposed to UV light. Nowadays, yellow and black colored LDPE-based films milk pouches are being used to prevent nutrients from degrading and to enhance shelf life [126]. For this reason, packaging materials that can absorb UV light are often used to protect milk and dairy products from degradation and extend their shelf life (Anju, 2020).

UV-screening packaging may improve the quality and shelf life of cheeses by inhibiting the oxidation of lipids, proteins, and pigments during storage (Jafarzadeh, Salehabadi, Mohammadi Nafchi, Oladzadabbasabadi, & Jafari, 2021). For instance, semolina flour films containing ZnO and kaolin NPs were shown to extend the shelf life of mozzarella cheese, which was attributed to its antioxidant and antimicrobial effects (Jafarzadeh, Rhim, Alias, Ariffin, & Mahmud, 2019). In another study, it was shown that chitosan films loaded with Santalum album essential oil reduced lipid oxidation in butter during storage (see Fig. 6 A) (Flórez, et al., 2022).

UV-blocking packaging materials can also be used to protect different kinds of beverages from photodegradation. For instance, the incorporation of bentonite into beverage packaging materials was shown to improve their UV-blocking properties without altering their transparency in the visible region (Passaretti, et al., 2019). A UV light barrier called Color MatrixTM UltimateTM® has been developed to protect the vitamins and minerals in coconut water from degrading when exposed to light (Avient, 2023). The company Eklipse® has developed shrink sleeve films with 99% light barrier properties for protecting beverages from degradation. Beverages can also be packaged in glass bottles that contain iron, carbon, sulfur, or chromium oxide as these substances can effectively block UV radiation. Biaxially-oriented polyethylene terephthalate (PET) bottles have been developed that can efficiently screen UV radiation while letting through visible radiation (Hostaphan, 2023). The incorporation of bentonite into packaging materials had been shown to improve the storage stability of flavored waters by blocking UV light (Passaretti, et al., 2019).

Edible oils contain monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fatty acids that are highly susceptible to oxidation when exposed to UV light, which leads to adverse effects on color, flavor, and nutrition (Gómez-Estaca, et al., 2014; Petkoska, Daniloski, D'Cunha, Naumovski, & Broach, 2021). UV-blocking films can be utilized to inhibit these undesirable effects. For instance, the introduction of lignin into soybean-based edible films was shown to inhibit the oxidation of soybean oil and fish oil by decreasing the oxidation rate (∼75%), which was mainly attributed to the UV screening effects of the lignin (Mohammad Zadeh, O'Keefe, & Kim, 2019). The incorporation of cellulose nanofibers into banana starch-based films was shown to screen UV radiation and increase the shelf life of cashew nut kernels. Their results displayed that cellulose nanofibers can block about 72% of UV radiation compared to the control film (Tibolla, et al., 2021). Furthermore, the introduction of grape seed extract into pectin/pullulan-based films was shown to screen UV radiation and protect peanuts from going rancid during 30 days of storage (Priyadarshi, Riahi, & Rhim, 2022). Curcumin-loaded polyurethane-gelatin films have also been shown to be able to retard lipid oxidation reactions in edible oils (T. Zhang et al., 2022) (see Fig. 6B).

Fresh fruits and vegetables can deteriorate when exposed to UV light due to various kinds of photooxidation reaction (Shang, et al., 2022). Moreover, fruit and vegetable juices can also degrade when exposed to UV light, which can lead to color fading, flavor changes, and nutrient loss (Oladzadabbasabadi, et al., 2022). This problem can be partially overcome by using UV-blocking packaging (Bayram, Ozkan, Kostka, Capanoglu, & Esatbeyoglu, 2021; Dilucia, Lacivita, Conte, & Del Nobile, 2020). For instance, the incorporation of functionalized waste wood fibers into polylactic acid-based films was shown to block UV radiation (∼100%) and delay the deterioration of fresh cherry tomatoes during storage (Shang, et al., 2022). Cinnamaldehyde-loaded corn starch films were also shown to provide good UV protection to strawberries during storage (S. Wan et al., 2023). The introduction of thymol into alginate-based films was also shown to reduce UV transmission and improve the quality and shelf life of freshly cut apples (J. Chen et al., 2021). As depicted in Fig. 7 A, PVA films loaded with carbon dots also exhibit strong UV-blocking properties, which helps with the preservation of strawberries during storage (Alaş, et al., 2022). Other researchers have shown that introducing carbon dots into egg albumin/chitosan films improved their UV screening properties and reduced the degradation of litchi during storage (Su, et al., 2023). PVA films containing waste tea residue carbon dots have been shown to exhibit strong UV-blocking properties, which improved the storage stability of grapefruit (see Fig. 7B) (Akshay S. Patil et al., 2020).

Exposure to UV radiation can promote the deterioration of bakery products due to photodegradation reactions, like lipid oxidation (Kotsianis, Giannou, Tzia, & Technology, 2002; Roy, Rhim, & Nutrition, 2021). Consequently, there is interest in developing UV-blocking packaging materials to protect these products. For instance, the incorporation of sulfur quantum dots into alginate-based films increased their UV barrier properties (82%) and helped prevent chemical and microbial deterioration of bread samples for up to 14 days (Riahi, et al., 2022). The incorporation of a plant Piper betle linn leaf extract into PVA/maltodextrin/agar-based films has also been shown to increase their UV-blocking properties, thereby extending the shelf life of bread samples over 45 days (Bui, Nguyen, Nguyen, Kim, & Nguyen, 2021). Similarly, the introduction of TiO2 NPs into PLA-based films was shown to increase the shelf life of rye bread through a similar mechanism (Soltani, Tavakolipour, & Tabari, 2023).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Challenges to the application of UV-shielding packaging materials
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in this article, UV-shielding packaging films may improve the quality, shelf life, and safety of foods by protecting them from damaging UV rays. However, there are serveral challenges that must be overcome before they can find widespread acceptance with the food industry, including material selection, scalability, recycling and disposal, and sustainability.

Selection of appropriate materials to assemble UV-shielding packaging materials is critical to their success. Ideally, these materials should be inexpensive, abundant, reliable, safe, sustainable, and functional. In particular, they should be able to effectively absorb or scatter UV light, without adversely affecting (or better still, improving) the functional performance of the packging material, such as its optical, mechanical, barrier, or preservative properties. It is also important to develop large-scale production processes that can economically fabricate UV-shield packaging materials at the quantities required by the food industry, otherwise they will not be commercially viable. Ideally, these packaging materials should be assembled from natural biodegradable components that will not adversely affect the environment or human health (Y. He et al., 2023; Zhang, Haque, & Naebe, 2023), such as proteins, polysaccharides, lipids, or natural extracts. Energy consumption during manufacturing is another issue, as the production of conventional UV-shielding films often requires considerable energy inputs, which contribute to carbon emissions and pollution (Gore and Prajapat, 2022; Patil, Nille, Kolekar, Sohn, & Gore, 2021; M. R. Silva et al., 2023). Moreover, UV-shielding packaging materials must undergo rigorous safety testing and certification. This includes assessing the safety of the films themselves, as well as the potential for migration of film additives into foods. Another challenging issue is that UV-shielding films and coatings both face color stability issues (yellowing) when exposed to UV light. The degree of impact depends on factors like composition, UV exposure, and environmental conditions. For UV-shielding applications like windows or packaging, the choice between films and coatings should consider the specific use. Coatings have higher UV absorbing compounds, leading to more yellowing, while films like bio-based films or Poly(ethylene terephthalate, PET) and polycarbonates are susceptible to outdoor yellowing without additives (M. R. F. Silva et al., 2023). The decision should be based on the intended application and the material's susceptibility to yellowing. UV absorber particles must have a nanometric size to effectively block UV rays, but achieving this size is challenging. Proper dispersion of the particles is crucial but hard to achieve. Some researchers are working on developing processing methods that use self-assembly to prevent the particles from clumping and agglomeration together. The text discusses the challenge of achieving effective UV protection with biopolymer nanocomposites. The goal is to maintain both mechanical strength and optical transparency while integrating multiple nanomaterials into the polymer matrix to block the complete UV region. However, this integration can impact the strength and transparency of the composites. There is also a focus on enhancing fluorescence/photoluminescence to emit visible light while absorbing UV radiation, paving the way for next-generation smart packaging materials (B.-H. Huang et al., 2021; L. Wang, Zhang, et al., 2022). The urgent task is to develop a sustainable synthesis pathway for nanomaterials covering the entire UV range at low concentrations, along with specific manufacturing methods for biopolymer nanocomposites. Finally, it will be necessary to develop an appropriate regulatory framework to guide the utilization of these innovative packaging materials in the food industry.

Photo-oxidation is a prominent factor contributing to the degradation of a wide range of food products, including meats, seafood, dairy products, edible oils, fruits, vegetables, bakery, and beverages. Photodegradation results in a reduction in nutritional content and sensory attributes of foodstuffs, and may even generate harmful substances. Ultraviolet radiation, particularly UV-A and UV-B wavelengths, can create significant challenges to the integrity of food products. To overcome these challenges, UV-shielding packaging materials have been developed. The adoption of these materials offers numeous benefits to food manufacturers and consumers. Firstly, these films provide an additional layer of protection for perishable food items, thereby reducing the risk of spoilage and extending their shelf life. This not only minimizes food waste but also enhances the overall sustainability of the food supply chain. Moreover, UV-shielding packaging films improve the sensory attributes and nutritional profile of packaged foods, thereby ensuring that consumers receive products that meet their expectations for quality and nutrition. Furthermore, the use of these films may improve safety of foods by reducing the generation of potentially toxic photodegradation reaction products during storage.

A variety of organic and inorganic additives may be included in biopolymer-based packaging materials to improve their UV-blocking properties and thereby enhance the quality and extend the shelf life of foods. These include biopolymers, phytochemicals, organic nanoparticles, and inorganic nanoparticles that can scatter or absorb UV light. However, it is often important that these substances do not block light strongly in the visible region of the electromagnetic spectrum, as this may adversely affect the perceived transparency or color of packaging materials. More research is therefore needed to identify economical, safe, and sustainable additives that absorb and/or scatter UV light strongly but not visible light. In addition, further research is required to quantify the impact of these UV screening agents on the barrier, mechanical, and preservative properties of packaging materials. In some cases, the incorporation of these screening agents may improve film properties but in other cases, they have the opposite effect. Consequently, an appropriate UV screening agent should be selected for each application. Finally, it will be necessary to create UV-blocking packaging materials that can be economically produced on the large scales needed for commercial applications and to show that these packaging materials do not adversely affect the environment or human health.
Not applicable.
Not applicable.
Not applicable.
